summary: Gruber pushes back against Ben Thompson's argument that Google's sale of Motorola to Lenovo was a shrewd strategic move rather than a costly mistake. He argues that Google's Motorola acquisition was a multi-billion-dollar failure, with toothless patents and unpopular devices. Gruber contends that Google strong-armed Samsung into compliance not through clever negotiation but through its control of proprietary Android apps. He notes that Samsung has no real leverage over Google without a viable alternative OS, and that the Motorola sale to Lenovo actually makes things worse for Samsung, not better. The piece challenges the narrative that Google masterfully turned lemons into lemonade with the Motorola deal.
key_points: Google's Motorola acquisition was a multi-billion-dollar mistake with toothless patents and unpopular devices, not a strategic masterstroke
Google forced Samsung into compliance by threatening to revoke access to proprietary apps like Maps, Gmail, and YouTube — not through negotiation
Samsung has zero leverage over Google because it lacks any feasible alternative OS strategy
Selling Motorola to Lenovo is actually bad news for Samsung, since Lenovo may prove a more competent competitor than Google-owned Motorola was
Google's continued hardware ambitions (Nest, Google Glass) undermine the narrative that the Motorola sale signals a retreat from consumer electronics
money_quotes: The idea that Google doesn't have its sights set on producing its own mass market consumer electronics would be a lot more compelling if they hadn't just bought Nest for $3 billion, and hadn't spent the last year hyping Google Glass.
They dumped Motorola because they couldn't make it work — Motorola's post-acquisition devices were, if anything, even less popular than the ones it made pre-acquisition.
Samsung has little to threaten Google with, and Google has much to threaten Samsung with.
Samsung is far and away the leading Android handset maker, but what leverage does that give them over Google? None, until and unless they have a feasible alternative OS strategy.
When Google tells Samsung to jump, Samsung asks 'How high?'
I fail to see how it was anything less than a multi-billion-dollar mistake. Their patents have proved toothless in court, and their devices have been disastrously unpopular.
I see not a single way that Google is better off for having owned Motorola, and several billions that it is worse.
key_insight: Google's Motorola acquisition was a straightforward multi-billion-dollar loss, not strategic lemonade, and Google's real power over the Android ecosystem comes from its control of proprietary apps, not hardware leverage.
